text: Hello and welcome to This is My Architecture. My name is Derek and I'm here today with Dave
from OpenFit. Welcome Dave, thanks for joining us today. Thank you. So before we dig into some of
the technical components here, can you tell us a little bit about OpenFit? Yeah, of course. OpenFit
was launched at the beginning of 2019. It's a digital streaming platform that integrates work
outs, nutrition, and wellness all in one place. Very cool. And I understand you're doing
full stack development and you're more or less all in on AWS. That's right, yeah. And
we're using a lot of AWS serverless components. Very cool, very cool. So I see here you're
using CodePipeline and we're sort of zoomed in on the continuous deployment aspect of your
build. Is that right? That's correct, yeah. Yeah? Very cool. So could you walk us through
a little bit? I understand this is kind of after the integration tests and unit tests
have run. We're really getting into the meat of how the build artifacts are created and deployed.
Yes, of course. So what are the stages here? So before the CodePipeline's run, there's a
PR, a pull request submitted to initiate the CodePipeline. Within that, that's where our continuous integration
runs. And within that PR, before the PR's run, there's integration tests, unit tests, Linten,
and the code review. So the PR initiates that when the code's ready to go to QA and hopefully production.
Got it. And I see this is kind of simplified a bit more than likely, but essentially you're building,
you're deploying an artifact to QA, you have a manual approval step, and then once that's approved,
you finally go to prod. Can you just kind of briefly describe, you know, the different stages here?
Yeah, I can. So before this actually happens, we have a Slack channel. We have a Slack channel for
each service, each of our microservices. Everybody who is involved in that process is in that Slack
channel. So when the PR comes through, there's a report built from that PR, and it's sent to the Slack
channel over here. And that's just a notification saying, hey, this happened, the build's been created.
It's a notification, and it's a report with commit IDs, which files have been modified,
and then the pull request number. Got it. And then next you're going to do the build,
and is that using code build? Yes, exactly. So this is where we start building the artifact.
It's an immutable artifact that we save, and then we save that to ECR. And then what happens next is we
pull that down from ECR, and we deploy it to QA using code build. Got it. Is there a notification step
here with Slack? Yeah. Throughout the pipeline, we send a notification at every step. So when the build
artifact is built, we get a notification saying it's built, and then the next notification comes through
when the QA environment's been built. Got it. Okay. So you've built the artifact, you've deployed to
your QA testing environment, and you've notified the Slack channel. And then can you tell us a little
bit about the proof steps? I think that's where sort of some of the chat ops component comes in here.
Exactly. So after the QA step, there'll be a message in the Slack channel, and the QA team
will know it's now time to look at this artifact, this deployment. So later on, once the QA team's
ready for this to go to production, they'll notify us in the Slack channel that everything looks good.
In the meantime, the approve function has already sent a Slack message
via an SNS to a Lambda to Slack. So this is an SNS provider. It sends an SNS payload to Lambda. This Lambda
uses a webhook into Slack and delivers a yes-no interactive message box.
Got it. So the build was successful. The deployable was successful. It's time to do an approval. You
want to pop up a yes-no box in the Slack chat. And so that's going to go through SNS to Lambda.
But I imagine that's asynchronous, right? It could take some amount of time before you get an answer.
So what happens after the yes-no box pops up?
Yeah, definitely. It is asynchronous. We load the payload in using this side of the notification.
And then when it's ready to go live, the QA team will notify us. And only the DevOps team can actually
click this button and there's permissions around that. So that will trigger the REST API on API Gateway,
execute it in Voka Lambda, and then tell the code pipeline to continue with its flow. And it deploys.
Then we move on to the next stage, which is deploy to production. And once again, it pulls the build
artifact down and then deploys to production. Got it. So that's a nice way for you to sort of be able to
quickly get those deployments out. How often are you doing this sort of deployment in a given day or a
given week? You know, some days it could be three to four times. And then other days, not quite as much.
But yeah, we can deploy very fast. We try and keep our code changes small so we can deploy frequently.
Awesome. So can you just talk a little bit about some of the benefits you've seen about adopting this
chat ops model? Why does the team like it so much? Of course. Yeah. I mean, when we was designing this
system, we thought about this part of the process. Because traditionally, it's held up deployments.
You know, you have to have releases and tickets in JIRA. Maybe everybody has to come into a room. And we
definitely didn't want to do that. Because using the test-driven development, we were able to push the
code out very fast because all the tests were happening before it even got to the deployment.
So that was the motivation behind the chat ops. Okay. Well, Dave, thank you so much for coming in
and sharing your architecture with us. And thank you for watching This Is My Architecture.
